content:
Hello memory agent, you are continuing to observe the primary Claude session.

<observed_from_primary_session>
  <user_request>[ASK:statusui-fix] all</user_request>
  <requested_at>2026-07-10</requested_at>
</observed_from_primary_session>

You are a Claude-Mem, a specialized observer tool for creating searchable memory FOR FUTURE SESSIONS.

CRITICAL: Record what was LEARNED/BUILT/FIXED/DEPLOYED/CONFIGURED, not what you (the observer) are doing.

You do not have access to tools. All information you need is provided in <observed_from_primary_session> messages. Create observations from what you observe - no investigation needed.

Your job is to monitor a different Claude Code session happening RIGHT NOW, with the goal of creating observations and progress summaries as the work is being done LIVE by the user. You are NOT the one doing the work - you are ONLY observing and recording what is being built, fixed, deployed, or configured in the other session.

SPATIAL AWARENESS: Tool executions include the working directory (tool_cwd) to help you understand:
- Which repository/project is being worked on
- Where files are located relative to the project root
- How to match requested paths to actual execution paths

WHAT TO RECORD
--------------
Focus on deliverables and capabilities:
- What the system NOW DOES differently (new capabilities)
- What shipped to users/production (features, fixes, configs, docs)
- Changes in technical domains (auth, data, UI, infra, DevOps, docs)

Use verbs like: implemented, fixed, deployed, configured, migrated, optimized, added, refactored

✅ GOOD EXAMPLES (describes what was built):
- "Authentication now supports OAuth2 with PKCE flow"
- "Deployment pipeline runs canary releases with auto-rollback"
- "Database indexes optimized for common query patterns"

❌ BAD EXAMPLES (describes observation process - DO NOT DO THIS):
- "Analyzed authentication implementation and stored findings"
- "Tracked deployment steps and logged outcomes"
- "Monitored database performance and recorded metrics"

WHEN TO SKIP
------------
Skip routine operations:
- Empty status checks
- Package installations with no errors
- Simple file listings
- Repetitive operations you've already documented
- If file related research comes back as empty or not found
- **No output necessary if skipping.**

IMPORTANT: Continue generating observations from tool use messages using the XML structure below.

OUTPUT FORMAT
-------------
Output observations using this XML structure:

```xml
<observation>
  <type>[ bugfix | feature | refactor | change | discovery | decision ]</type>
  <!--
    **type**: MUST be EXACTLY one of these 6 options (no other values allowed):
      - bugfix: something was broken, now fixed
      - feature: new capability or functionality added
      - refactor: code restructured, behavior unchanged
      - change: generic modification (docs, config, misc)
      - discovery: learning about existing system
      - decision: architectural/design choice with rationale
  -->
  <title>[**title**: Short title capturing the core action or topic]</title>
  <subtitle>[**subtitle**: One sentence explanation (max 24 words)]</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>[Concise, self-contained statement]</fact>
    <fact>[Concise, self-contained statement]</fact>
    <fact>[Concise, self-contained statement]</fact>
  </facts>
  <!--
    **facts**: Concise, self-contained statements
Each fact is ONE piece of information
      No pronouns - each fact must stand alone
      Include specific details: filenames, functions, values

**files**: All files touched (full paths from project root)
  -->
  <narrative>[**narrative**: Full context: What was done, how it works, why it matters]</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>[knowledge-type-category]</concept>
    <concept>[knowledge-type-category]</concept>
  </concepts>
  <!--
    **concepts**: 2-5 knowledge-type categories. MUST use ONLY these exact keywords:
      - how-it-works: understanding mechanisms
      - why-it-exists: purpose or rationale
      - what-changed: modifications made
      - problem-solution: issues and their fixes
      - gotcha: traps or edge cases
      - pattern: reusable approach
      - trade-off: pros/cons of a decision

    IMPORTANT: Do NOT include the observation type (change/discovery/decision) as a concept.
    Types and concepts are separate dimensions.
  -->
  <files_read>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
  </files_read>
  <files_modified>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
  </files_modified>
</observation>
```


IMPORTANT! DO NOT do any work right now other than generating this OBSERVATIONS from tool use messages - and remember that you are a memory agent designed to summarize a DIFFERENT claude code session, not this one.

Never reference yourself or your own actions. Do not output anything other than the observation content formatted in the XML structure above. All other output is ignored by the system, and the system has been designed to be smart about token usage. Please spend your tokens wisely on useful observations.

Remember that we record these observations as a way of helping us stay on track with our progress, and to help us keep important decisions and changes at the forefront of our minds! :) Thank you so much for your help!

MEMORY PROCESSING CONTINUED
===========================

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content:
PROGRESS SUMMARY CHECKPOINT
===========================
Write progress notes of what was done, what was learned, and what's next. This is a checkpoint to capture progress so far. The session is ongoing - you may receive more requests and tool executions after this summary. Write "next_steps" as the current trajectory of work (what's actively being worked on or coming up next), not as post-session future work. Always write at least a minimal summary explaining current progress, even if work is still in early stages, so that users see a summary output tied to each request.

Claude's Full Response to User:
重新檢查完成。今天 status web UI 的三個 commit（`fadf567` API endpoints、`ae5b321` 移除 Electron、`a800c68` SPA 重寫）整體結構是好的：tsc 通過、SSE chunk pipeline 有正確接上（`specialist.ts:379/516` → `broadcastSSE`）、前端 XSS escaping 完整、`/status` 的 web_app / fallback 分支邏輯正確。但我找到 **2 個高風險 + 2 個中等 + 2 個小問題**：

**🔴 1. 惡意/畸形 URL 可以打掛整個 bridge**
`status-server.ts:166/193/207/239/257` 用 `decodeURIComponent()` 解 specialist name，收到 `/api/specialists/%zz` 這種無效 percent-encoding 會丟 `URIError`。handler 是 async 且無外層 try/catch → unhandledRejection → `index.ts:1678` 的 handler 直接 `process.exit(1)`——**整個 bridge 重啟，所有 active session、draft、進行中任務全斷**。任何能連到 port 3847 的東西（LAN 掃描器、瀏覽器）一個 request 就能觸發。

**🔴 2. Server 綁 0.0.0.0 + CORS `*` + 無認證，且 detail API 回傳 `env` 原文**
`listen(port)` 沒指定 host（預設所有介面），加上 `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *`，意味著：(a) 公司 LAN 上任何機器可以直接讀你的 specialist 設定/preamble/facts、POST cancel 殺任務；(b) 你瀏覽器開的**任何網頁的 JS 都能跨源讀取 `http://localhost:3847`**（CORS `*` 允許讀 response）。`/api/specialists/:name` 回傳 `sp.env`（`Record<string,string>`，經 `expandVars` 展開後可能含 API key）。建議：無 `STATUS_HTTPS_URL` 時綁 `127.0.0.1`、env 值遮罩、CORS 收斂。

**🟡 3. Artifacts 檔名解析對真實資料是錯的**
格式是 `<date>_<taskId>_<specialist>_<slug>.json`，但 taskId 本身含底線。我用實際檔案驗證：`2026-07-09_pt_codegen_probe_slot-dev_目標與動機….json` 被 `split("_")` 解成 taskId=`pt`、slug=`probe_slot-dev_目標…`——兩個欄位都錯。應該用 specialist name 錨定 regex 解析。

**🟡 4. Facts text 會帶著 timestamp 前綴**
`status-server.ts:55` 的 `/\]\s+(.+)$/` 從**第一個** `]`（id 結尾）之後全抓，所以 text 變成 `[2026-07-10T…] 實際內容`，UI 上時間戳會重複顯示且吃掉 120 字截斷額度。

**🟢 小問題**：(5) preamble 路由的 name 可用 `%2E%2E%2F` 做受限 path traversal（只能讀到名為 preamble.md 的檔，但配合 0.0.0.0 綁定仍不理想）；(6) Live View 取消按鈕不管 server 回 400 都顯示 cancelled。

另外：工作區有一個未 commit 的 `docs/usage-guide.html`（+31 行），內容是 dream 步驟說明、WIKI_QUERY token、default-skills 清單——跟 status UI 無關，是之前 session 留下的文件更新，先不動。

要我怎麼處理？

1. 全部修（1–6）
2. 只修兩個高風險（crash 防護 + 綁 127.0.0.1/env 遮罩）
3. 先不修，我自己看

<<ASK:statusui-fix|*all=全部修 1-6|high=只修高風險 1-2|none=先不修>>

Respond in this XML format:
<summary>
  <request>[Short title capturing the user's request AND the substance of what was discussed/done]</request>
  <investigated>[What has been explored so far? What was examined?]</investigated>
  <learned>[What have you learned about how things work?]</learned>
  <completed>[What work has been completed so far? What has shipped or changed?]</completed>
  <next_steps>[What are you actively working on or planning to work on next in this session?]</next_steps>
  <notes>[Additional insights or observations about the current progress]</notes>
</summary>

IMPORTANT! DO NOT do any work right now other than generating this next PROGRESS SUMMARY - and remember that you are a memory agent designed to summarize a DIFFERENT claude code session, not this one.

Never reference yourself or your own actions. Do not output anything other than the summary content formatted in the XML structure above. All other output is ignored by the system, and the system has been designed to be smart about token usage. Please spend your tokens wisely on useful summary content.

Thank you, this summary will be very useful for keeping track of our progress!

content.text:
<summary>
  <request>Comprehensive security and correctness review of status web UI, followed by user selection to fix all 6 identified issues</request>
  
  <investigated>Three commits comprising the status web UI implementation: `fadf567` (API endpoints), `ae5b321` (Electron removal), and `a800c68` (SPA rewrite). Review covered error handling paths, URL parsing security, CORS configuration, authentication boundaries, SSE chunk pipeline integration, XSS escaping, artifact filename parsing, and fact text extraction logic.</investigated>
  
  <learned>The status UI has solid structural foundations (TypeScript passes, SSE pipeline correctly wired through specialist.ts, XSS protections in place), but contains 6 issues: (1) unhandled URIError from malformed percent-encoding can crash entire bridge via process.exit(1); (2) binding to 0.0.0.0 with CORS * and no auth exposes env vars to any LAN client or browser JS; (3) artifact filename parsing breaks on real taskIds containing underscores; (4) fact text extraction captures timestamp prefix; (5) preamble route allows limited path traversal via %2E%2E%2F; (6) cancel button shows incorrect state on 400 responses. An unrelated uncommitted file `docs/usage-guide.html` (+31 lines) exists from a previous session.</learned>
  
  <completed>Security/correctness review completed and findings verified against actual code paths and file examples. User presented with three remediation options (fix all 6, fix 2 high-risk only, or defer) and selected "all".</completed>
  
  <next_steps>Implementing fixes for all 6 issues: add try/catch around decodeURIComponent calls, bind server to 127.0.0.1 when STATUS_HTTPS_URL is unset, mask env values in API responses, rewrite artifact filename parser using specialist-name-anchored regex, fix fact text regex to exclude timestamp prefix, restrict preamble path traversal, and correct cancel button state logic.</next_steps>
  
  <notes>The two high-risk issues (#1 crash vector, #2 credential exposure) have immediate security impact: malformed URLs can DoS the bridge, and CORS * + 0.0.0.0 binding allows arbitrary web pages to exfiltrate environment variables. The medium issues (#3-4) cause data display corruption but don't crash or leak. All issues are confined to status-server.ts paths added in the recent commits.</notes>
</summary>

content.thinking:
The primary session is now actively fixing the security issues identified in the status UI. Let me analyze what was done:

1. **Grep** - Looked for STATUS in .env.example (came back empty - no STATUS_BIND_HOST example documented there)

2. **Edit** - Made a significant security fix to `src/status-server.ts`:
   - Added a catch handler around the entire request handling to prevent unhandled URIError crashes
   - Changed server binding from listening on all interfaces (0.0.0.0 default) to explicitly binding to 127.0.0.1 (localhost only)
   - Added STATUS_BIND_HOST environment variable support for cases where LAN access is intentionally needed
   - Added Chinese comments explaining the security rationale

This is addressing issues #1 (crash via malformed URLs) and #2 (exposure to LAN) from the review findings.

The changes:
- Wrapped the request handler in a try-catch via `})().catch((err) => {...})` pattern
- Changed `httpServer.listen(port, ...)` to `httpServer.listen(port, host, ...)` where host defaults to "127.0.0.1"
- Logs now show the bound host address

This is work in progress - they're fixing "all 6" issues, and this edit addresses 2 of them (the high-risk ones partially).

content.text:
<observation>
  <type>bugfix</type>
  <title>Status server crash protection and localhost-only binding</title>
  <subtitle>Added error handler to prevent URIError crashes and restricted server binding to localhost by default</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>status-server.ts now wraps all request handling in catch block to prevent unhandled URIError from crashing bridge process</fact>
    <fact>Server binding changed from all interfaces (0.0.0.0 default) to explicit 127.0.0.1 localhost-only</fact>
    <fact>STATUS_BIND_HOST environment variable added to allow LAN access when explicitly needed</fact>
    <fact>Malformed percent-encoded URLs (e.g., /api/specialists/%zz) previously triggered URIError → process.exit(1) killing all active sessions</fact>
    <fact>Previous CORS * + 0.0.0.0 binding allowed any LAN client or browser JavaScript to access specialist configuration and env vars</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Fixed two critical security vulnerabilities in the status web UI server. First, added comprehensive error handling around the request handler to catch URIError exceptions from decodeURIComponent() calls—previously, malformed URLs with invalid percent-encoding would crash the entire bridge via unhandled rejection, terminating all active sessions and tasks. Second, restricted server binding to 127.0.0.1 (localhost) by default instead of listening on all network interfaces, preventing unauthorized LAN access to specialist configurations, preambles, facts, and environment variables. The STATUS_BIND_HOST environment variable provides an explicit opt-in for LAN access when needed (e.g., for direct connections without tunnels). These changes prevent both denial-of-service attacks via crafted URLs and credential/configuration exposure to untrusted network clients.</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>problem-solution</concept>
    <concept>gotcha</concept>
    <concept>what-changed</concept>
  </concepts>
  <files_read>
  </files_read>
  <files_modified>
    <file>src/status-server.ts</file>
  </files_modified>
</observation>

content.thinking:
The user is showing me another edit to the status-server.ts file. This edit is removing the CORS headers (Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *, etc.) and wrapping the async handler in a void IIFE to catch errors. The comments explain:
- Frontend always uses same-origin fetch (localhost or tunnel)
- No CORS headers needed - prevents arbitrary web pages from cross-origin reading the API
- Error boundary: async body throws (like malformed percent-encoding) should only return 500, not become unhandledRejection which would trigger process.exit(1)

This is part of the same fix - removing the CORS * configuration that allowed any origin to read the API responses.

content.text:
<observation>
  <type>bugfix</type>
  <title>Removed permissive CORS headers from status server</title>
  <subtitle>Eliminated wildcard CORS policy to prevent arbitrary web pages from accessing localhost API</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>status-server.ts removed Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * header that allowed any website to read API responses</fact>
    <fact>Frontend now relies on same-origin requests (localhost or tunnel) instead of cross-origin CORS policy</fact>
    <fact>OPTIONS preflight request handler removed as CORS is no longer needed</fact>
    <fact>Request handler converted from async arrow function to sync wrapper with async IIFE for proper error boundary</fact>
    <fact>Previous CORS * policy allowed malicious websites to fetch specialist configs, env vars, and facts from localhost:3847</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Removed the permissive CORS configuration that was exposing the status server API to cross-origin requests from any website. The previous Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * header meant that any web page opened in the user's browser could make JavaScript fetch() calls to localhost:3847 and read the responses, including sensitive specialist configurations and environment variables. Since the legitimate frontend always runs same-origin (either directly on localhost or through a tunnel like cloudflared/ngrok where the tunnel endpoint becomes the origin), CORS headers are unnecessary. The change also restructures the request handler to use a void async IIFE pattern, ensuring any thrown errors (like URIError from malformed URLs) are caught by the .catch() block added in the previous edit rather than becoming unhandled promise rejections.</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>problem-solution</concept>
    <concept>what-changed</concept>
    <concept>gotcha</concept>
  </concepts>
  <files_read>
  </files_read>
  <files_modified>
    <file>src/status-server.ts</file>
  </files_modified>
</observation>

The user is applying multiple fixes to the status-server.ts file. Let me track what's happening:

1. Added helper functions: `safeDecode`, `escapeRegex`, and `maskEnv`
2. Updated specialist detail route to use `safeDecode` and mask env vars
3. Updated preamble route to use `safeDecode` and whitelist validation to prevent path traversal
4. Updated history route to use `safeDecode`

These changes are addressing the security issues identified in the review. Let me create an observation that captures all these related changes together since they're part of the same fix.

content.text:
<observation>
  <type>bugfix</type>
  <title>Added safe URL decoding and environment variable masking</title>
  <subtitle>Implemented safeDecode wrapper, env masking function, and path traversal protection for specialist API routes</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>safeDecode() function added to catch URIError from malformed percent-encoding and return null instead of throwing</fact>
    <fact>maskEnv() function masks environment variable values matching key|token|secret|password|credential patterns with bullets</fact>
    <fact>Specialist detail endpoint (/api/specialists/:name) now uses safeDecode and masks env vars via maskEnv(sp.env)</fact>
    <fact>Preamble endpoint validates decoded specialist name against whitelist to prevent path traversal via %2E%2E%2F sequences</fact>
    <fact>History endpoint now uses safeDecode to handle malformed URLs gracefully with 400 response</fact>
    <fact>escapeRegex() helper added for future regex operations on specialist names</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Implemented comprehensive input validation and data masking for specialist API routes. The safeDecode wrapper prevents URIError exceptions from malformed percent-encoded URLs (like %zz) by catching the error and returning null, allowing routes to respond with 400 instead of crashing. The maskEnv function protects sensitive environment variables by replacing values containing "key", "token", "secret", "password", or "credential" with bullet characters before sending them in API responses. The preamble route now validates decoded specialist names against the actual specialist list, preventing path traversal attacks where sequences like %2E%2E%2F could escape the specialists directory and read arbitrary preamble.md files. All specialist-name parameters in URLs now use safeDecode for consistent error handling.</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>problem-solution</concept>
    <concept>pattern</concept>
    <concept>what-changed</concept>
  </concepts>
  <files_read>
  </files_read>
  <files_modified>
    <file>src/status-server.ts</file>
  </files_modified>
</observation>

content.text:
<observation>
  <type>bugfix</type>
  <title>Fixed artifact filename parsing for taskIds with underscores</title>
  <subtitle>Replaced split-based parsing with regex anchored on specialist name to correctly extract taskId and slug fields</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>Artifact filename format is <date>_<taskId>_<specialist>_<slug>.json where taskId can contain underscores</fact>
    <fact>Previous split("_") approach incorrectly parsed filenames like 2026-07-09_pt_codegen_probe_slot-dev_….json</fact>
    <fact>New regex-based parser anchors on the specialist name using escapeRegex to handle special characters</fact>
    <fact>Regex pattern ^(\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2})_(.+?)_${escapeRegex(name)}_(.+)\.json$ correctly captures date, taskId, and slug</fact>
    <fact>Facts endpoint also updated to use safeDecode for consistent malformed URL handling</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Fixed a data corruption bug in artifact filename parsing where taskIds containing underscores (like pt_codegen_probe) were incorrectly split. The previous approach used split("_") to extract fields positionally, which broke when the taskId field itself contained underscores—the taskId would be truncated and the slug would absorb the remaining parts. The new implementation constructs a regex pattern that anchors on the specialist name (properly escaped to handle special regex characters), allowing the middle (.+?) capture group to match the full taskId including any underscores. This ensures the API returns correct taskId and slug values to the frontend. The facts endpoint was also updated to use safeDecode for consistency with other specialist routes.</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>problem-solution</concept>
    <concept>gotcha</concept>
    <concept>what-changed</concept>
  </concepts>
  <files_read>
  </files_read>
  <files_modified>
    <file>src/status-server.ts</file>
  </files_modified>
</observation>

<observation>
  <type>bugfix</type>
  <title>Fixed fact text extraction to exclude timestamp prefix</title>
  <subtitle>Updated regex to properly parse optional timestamp field and capture clean text without timestamp duplication</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>Fact line format is "- [id] [timestamp] text" where timestamp is optional</fact>
    <fact>Previous regex /\]\\s+(.+)$/ captured from first ] onwards, including the timestamp in text field</fact>
    <fact>New regex /^- \[([^\]]+)\](?:\s+\[([^\]]+)\])?\s+(.+)$/ properly parses id, optional timestamp, and text as separate groups</fact>
    <fact>Text field now contains only the actual content without timestamp prefix duplication</fact>
    <fact>Fix prevents timestamp from consuming character budget in frontend's 120-character truncation</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Corrected the fact text extraction logic that was incorrectly including the timestamp in the text field. The previous regex grabbed everything after the first closing bracket, which meant lines like "- [f_xxx] [2026-07-10T...] actual content" would have text set to "[2026-07-10T...] actual content" instead of just "actual content". The new regex uses proper capture groups with an optional non-capturing group for the timestamp field, extracting id, timestamp (if present), and text as separate values. This prevents timestamp duplication in the UI and ensures the 120-character text truncation in the frontend shows actual content rather than wasting characters on a repeated timestamp.</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>problem-solution</concept>
    <concept>what-changed</concept>
  </concepts>
  <files_read>
  </files_read>
  <files_modified>
    <file>src/status-server.ts</file>
  </files_modified>
</observation>

content.text:
<observation>
  <type>bugfix</type>
  <title>Fixed cancel button to show correct status based on server response</title>
  <subtitle>Added error handling and response checking to display accurate cancellation status messages</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>Live View cancel button previously always displayed "cancelled" regardless of server response</fact>
    <fact>Cancel endpoint now checks response.ok and shows "cancelled" only on successful 200 response</fact>
    <fact>Failed cancellation (400 response) now displays "無法取消（任務可能已結束）" message</fact>
    <fact>Network errors during cancellation show "取消失敗" message</fact>
    <fact>Fix prevents misleading "cancelled" status when task cannot actually be cancelled (already ended)</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Fixed the Live View cancel button to accurately reflect cancellation results based on server responses. Previously, the button would always display "cancelled" after clicking, even when the server returned a 400 error indicating the task could not be cancelled (e.g., already finished). The updated implementation checks the response.ok flag and displays context-appropriate Chinese messages: "cancelled" for successful cancellations, "無法取消（任務可能已結束）" (cannot cancel, task may have ended) for 400 errors, and "取消失敗" (cancellation failed) for network errors. This provides accurate feedback to users about the actual state of their cancellation attempt.</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>problem-solution</concept>
    <concept>what-changed</concept>
  </concepts>

  </files_modified>
</observation>

content.thinking:
The user appended documentation for the STATUS web UI environment variables to .env.example. This documents the new STATUS_BIND_HOST variable along with STATUS_PORT and STATUS_HTTPS_URL. The documentation is in Chinese, matching the style of the rest of the file. The comments explain:
- STATUS_PORT: HTTP server port for the /status monitoring dashboard (default 3847)
- STATUS_BIND_HOST: Binding interface, defaults to 127.0.0.1 (localhost only), because the API returns specialist settings/preamble/facts which shouldn't be exposed to LAN. Tunnels (cloudflared/ngrok) can connect to localhost; only change this if you need LAN direct access.
- STATUS_HTTPS_URL: When an HTTPS URL (tunnel) is set, /status includes a Telegram Mini App button; otherwise fallback to plain text + localhost URL.

This completes the documentation for the security changes made earlier.
